title: W2: Data for LLMs and Evaluation
blurb: High priority. The exams repeatedly ask how to curate web-scale data and how perplexity relates to log-likelihood.

knowledge_points.title: Pretraining Data Curation
knowledge_points.recap: Common Crawl is raw and noisy. The course emphasizes heuristic filters, deduplication, source mixing/weighting, ML-based quality filters, and rewriting low-quality data.

questions.q: Name common strategies for curating large-scale pretraining data.
questions.answer: Valid strategies include heuristic-based filtering, deduplication, mixing and weighting data sources, machine-learning-based quality filtering, and rewriting low-quality data with LLMs.
questions.extend: For 3-point questions, give three distinct strategies and one short phrase explaining each.

questions.q: You have Common Crawl and 100k representative scientific Q&A pairs. How would you filter for scientific Q&A pretraining?
questions.answer: Use the scientific Q&A pairs as positive high-quality/domain-relevant examples and sample Common Crawl documents as negative examples. Balance the positive and negative training set, train a binary classifier for quality/domain relevance, and keep Common Crawl documents classified as suitable for scientific Q&A.
questions.extend: The scoring points are: positives from the target domain, sampled negatives from Common Crawl, balanced classifier training, then filtering.

knowledge_points.title: Perplexity and Evaluation
knowledge_points.recap: Perplexity is the exponentiated average negative log-probability. Lower perplexity means the model assigns higher probability to the observed sequence.

questions.q: Define perplexity for an autoregressive LM and explain why minimizing it maximizes sequence log-probability.
questions.answer: For tokens $t_1,\ldots,t_N$, perplexity = $\exp\left(-\frac{1}{N}\sum_i \log p_\theta(t_i\mid t_{<i})\right)$. By the chain rule, $\log p_\theta(t_1,\ldots,t_N)=\sum_i \log p_\theta(t_i\mid t_{<i})$. Since exp is monotone and the factor $-1/N$ reverses the sign, minimizing perplexity is equivalent to maximizing sequence log-probability.
questions.extend: This is a near-guaranteed formula question; keep the chain-rule line on your sheet.

questions.q: What is the maximum possible perplexity for a vocabulary of size 10, and when is it attained?
questions.answer: The maximum is 10, attained by the uniform distribution over the 10 tokens, where each token has probability $1/10$ and perplexity becomes $\exp(\log 10)=10$.
questions.extend: More generally, for a vocabulary of size v, uniform uncertainty gives perplexity v.

questions.q: Name two ways to evaluate a pretrained LLM without fine-tuning.
questions.answer: Two accepted methods are perplexity on held-out text and benchmark evaluation using few-shot prompting. Human evaluation is also valid but expensive and subjective.
questions.extend: Do not describe supervised fine-tuning here; the question asks about evaluation without changing weights.
